Hi Priscilla. Well, this is my big international conference week. We will have probably over 1000 people, actually, in Anaheim in our conference this year- at least 700 directors from all over the world. It is like going to a United Nations conference because there are all these flags and accents.

It is really amazing. I look back. 30 years ago almost – we are coming up on our 30th anniversary- I just never would have guessed. Events like this are awe inspiring. It will be a good week.

Priscilla:
Great. Well, tell us what you have for today.

Ivan:
Well, one of the directors is a good friend of mine and a business partner of mine in the Referral Institute. His name is Mike Macedonio. Mike and I had a great conversation a while back about what makes someone referable. Mike, by the way, is the President of the Referral Institute. If you have a chance, go to ReferralInstitute.com, and get information about the Referral Institute. They are looking for trainers around the world. So if that looks like it is something that is of interest to you, take a look at their website and connect with Mike.

Anyway, Mike was explaining to me why he feels that there are only a few criteria that need to be met to make people referable by him. I thought that might make an interesting podcast topic.

The first criteria that he said an individual must have is that they have to be an expert at what they do. Mike says that when he trains people, he trains them to look for people who have invested in learning their trade and most importantly, continue to invest to master their trade.

Do they specialize in a certain area? What achievements have they obtained in that area of expertise? I think you really have to get to know the person reasonably well to make sure that they are constantly investing in what it is that they do so that they can continue to be better and better at what they do. That makes it, obviously, more comfortable for him to refer people to that individual because they are constantly honing out their saw, sharpening their ax, so to speak.

Another one of Mike’s requirements is that the person is passionate about what they do. This especially makes a lot of sense to me because if you are not passionate about what you do, how can you expect other people to get excited about working on your behalf, about referring people to you? You just have to be passionate. Nothing great in life has ever been accomplished without a little bit of excitement, so you want people who are passionate about what they do. That is certainly a criteria for me and it is one of the ones that Mike from the Referral Institute seems to support as well.

Mike’s last criterion stipulates that the person who he is referring understands and honors the referral process. That is an important one. They understand and honor the referral process. More specifically, Mike wants to make sure that the person receiving the referral understands that their responsibility is that when they receive a referral, to make the person who gave the referral look great. That’s interesting. I bet we don’t do that in BNI as often as we could. That is, to edify the person who gave the referral to the individual that you are talking about and are about to do business with.

So if Mike were to give me a referral and I went to that referral, the first thing that I should do is edify Mike and talk about how good he is at what he does before I start to talk about what I do. Mike feels that as long as the people who he gives referrals to are doing this for him, he can remain confident that his reputation is going to be protected, and it also compels him to continue to give those referrals.

That is an important concept. You want to edify the people that are giving you referrals to the referrals themselves. It is something that I don’t think we train on enough in BNI. I am really glad that I had this conversation with him because I think it is an important conversation to have. I would love to see education coordinators sharing this at chapters.

Mike’s list of qualifications that makes a person referable is really short but I think it is very powerful. We both agreed that we should expect others to evaluate our referrability by these same criteria. Are we invested experts? Do we continue to invest in our trade and learning more about our trade? Are we passionate about we do? Are we practicing what we preach? Do we make our referral sources look great?

It is important to walk the talk. You know the old saying that what you do thunders above your head so loudly that I cannot hear the words you speak. So walking the talk means doing these things for others, not just expecting them. But I think it is also important, particularly in the BNI context, to have the conversation with everyone. And maybe in our 60 second presentations, take a week to talk about how we are investing in our ongoing education so that everybody knows what you are doing.

How are you passionate about your business? That might make a great 60 second presentation. What are you passionate about with your business? And how do you go about edifying the people who give you referrals? Give a few referrals. All of these are great things to do, I think, publicly at a meeting and I think this is a great topic of discussion for an education coordinator.

So what I would ask, and Priscilla, I would love for you to offer any other input you have got, but for those of you listening, I would ask what makes people referable by you? Is there something in addition to the few criteria that you think are important? And maybe not just run of the mill kind of stuff, like they do a good job. That is a given. What makes people referable by you? I would love to have you put that on the podcast here at BNIpodcast.com

Priscilla, is there anything you would add to that, or is there something you have?

Priscilla:
Yeah, I did read your blog on that and I thought that what Mike had meant was that when you perform your duty or you respond to the person who you are contacting, the referral- not the source but the person who you are going to work for- that you do such a good job that it makes the other person look good. So you have that in mind and you really do everything correctly and with great service so that the person who referred you looks like a genius. I thought that was what he meant.

Ivan:
Yeah. I would agree with that statement. I don’t think that is what he meant with that statement. I think he would agree with your statement, but I don’t believe that is what he meant by his statement. What he is really talking about is edifying that person right from the very beginning so that if Mike referred me to someone- and he has referred me to speak for other organizations- I would go and I would talk to that organization about speaking on networking and one of the first things that I would do is edify Mike to that organization and say, “Look, if Mike referred me, I take that seriously because Mike is one of the best referral trainers I have ever known in my life. He is really good at what he does, so if he refers a customer to me, a prospective client, I want to make sure to take good care of him because Mike is very good at what he does.”

I would edify him in some way. That makes the connection because if they accepted Mike’s recommendation, they must like him. That supports their position that it was a good idea to take Mike’s referral because the first thing I do is talk about how good Mike is. Now we have something in common that we can talk about.

I do this with prospective clients and prospective business associates. It is a real ice breaker because you begin the dialogue by talking about something you both know in a positive way. Does that make sense?

Priscilla:
Yeah, that makes sense.

Ivan:
That said, what you are also saying is you want to do a great job so that you make the person who referred you look good. That is a given. If you don’t do that, then you are going to have some real problems.

That is all I have for today.

Priscilla:
Okay, great.Thank you, Dr. Misner. Well, that is it for this week, and I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the GlobalNetworkingShow.com, hosted by Andy Lopata and Dr. Ivan Misner. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Dr. Misner shares the criteria that Mike Macedonio from the Referral Institute uses in deciding whether someone is referable.

title.rendered: Episode 380: Finding Your Inner Networker

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Carlos Cooper, one of the Mentor Code authors, joins Dr. Misner today to talk about their new book, I Love Networking. Carlos was one of those people who used to hate networking, but thanks to BNI, he came to enjoy networking.

This book is perfect for any chapter that wants to mentor members in a formal way. It takes you step-by-step through the first few months of membership in BNI, both from the new member’s perspective and from the mentor’s perspective. The new member in the story starts out as someone who hates networking but discovers his inner networker and learns to love networking. (Based on Carlos Cooper’s own experience.)

Both new and experienced BNI members can benefit from reading this short guide to success in networking. Find your inner networker and learn to love networking!

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 380 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by the NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi Priscilla. I am doing great. I am in Los Angeles at BNI headquarters this week. We are preparing for our big international conference, where we bring particularly Directors from around the world. We are expecting about 1000 people.

Priscilla:
Wow. That is great.

Ivan:
I am really excited. I have someone on the call who I am interviewing today, Carlos Cooper. Carlos graduated from the University of Virginia. He is a former Marine. He is a prolific author. His is the author of the Mentor Code series. The book that we are going to be talking about is from the Mentor Code. He has written books on the Chronicles of Benjamin Dragon, Self Publishing Success School, the Right Practice, and he has written a book with me called I Love Networking.

I Love Networking’s subtitle is Finding Your Inner Networker, the subject today. Carlos, welcome to the BNI podcast.

Carlos:
Thanks for having me. I appreciate it.

Ivan:
It is great having you. You have been a member of BNI for a number of years on the Leadership Team. Your Directors love you, by the way. They thought you were an amazing member. But you decided to go full time as a writer doing books. Is that working out well for you?

Carlos:
It is working out very well. 2014 has been a very big year. I know I will be able to tell a little bit of my story later, but I really equate it all back to BNI. Even though I am not part of it right now, it kind of changed my life, so I am glad to be here today and I am glad to tell a little bit of that story.

Ivan:
Well, we are glad to have you. We hate to see some people leave, but some professions are not necessarily as conducive. Writing for a living- not editorial writing for business- but fiction or not fiction writing. There is not a big call for referrals for those as a rule.

But listen, you had a great idea for this book, I Love Networking: Finding Your Inner Networker. Where did the idea come from, because this is all your idea. My name is on there, but it was totally your idea.

Carlos:
Well, you say that, but the idea actually came from you because you started BNI. You and I have kind of joked about the I Love Networking title because so many people say that they hate networking. I was one of those people. I told you that. I went to BNI the first time kicking and screaming because I had been to other events and I really couldn’t stand it.

I was a business owner and had to do something. And then, low and behold, I was sitting there in a BNI meeting going, hey, nobody is pestering me. Everybody is very nice and they are trying to help me. So that is really- I kind of want to tell a little bit of that story. It is a story that I know you have heard a 1000 times and I know I have heard a couple hundred. It’s a common story that people come into thinking it’s all about one thing, but really it’s something completely different. It is all about making friends.

Ivan:
Yeah. You know, there is a lot in here. I want to circle back around to this concept that I am going to bring up here. It is mentoring. Of course, you wrote the Mentor Code series, which this is part of. I want to come back to that because if you are listening to this podcast, this book is perfect for any chapter that wants to mentor members in a formal way.

So let me come back around to that topic. Why do you think that this book is perfect for leaders, and particularly for BNI.

Carlos:
Like you said, I am a former Marine, so I like a to the point story, something simple that is easy to digest, you know. Under 100 pages. It kind of takes you step by step through, okay, I am thinking about joining and then okay, if you are a mentor, maybe these are the ways that you should usher a new member through the first few months, which you know as well as I do that the first few months are the hardest for a new member to go through.

It just kind of walks you through both sides of , “Hey, I am the new guy. What do I need to do?” Even for seasoned people who have been around for a while, it is a great thing for ideas on how can I help other people and how can I help myself?

Ivan:
Yeah, and it’s an easy read. It is about 78 pages, including notes pages, so it’s a pretty easy read and it is a parable. It is done as a story about somebody who hates networking. You and I talked about this. Should we call it I Hate Networking? I Love Networking? What were we going to do?

The person in this story absolutely hates networking. Someone takes him under his wing, brings him to BNI and really teaches him how to network effectively and it’s really the story of that process. What would you add?

Carlos:
Exactly. Exactly. Luckily, I had mentors who showed me how to do it the right way or else I probably would have screwed up if I had done it on my own.

Ivan:
Yeah. You know what, we all learn how to do this. I certainly didn’t know how to network when I started BNI. I was clueless. One of the things that I did was try to fill the room with people who were more successful than me so that I could go to school on them because I didn’t know what I was doing. So having mentors is so important. What I love about the concept of this book is showing people how they can evolve and develop the skills. Also, it really teaches someone else who wants to mentor someone, I think.

When you read it, you don’t necessarily think that, but it is almost a primer for how to be a good mentor to someone in a BNI group.

Carlos:
Yeah, I almost thing that you should read it twice if not more. You read it when you are a new member and you read it when you are in the mentor coordinator role. Three years in, you are not going to remember what it felt like to be the new guy or the new girl. To be able to read something to teach yourself is kind of a good tool to have.

Ivan:
Yeah. Now, you mentioned the Mentor Coordinator. Some chapters have them. Some don’t. If your chapter doesn’t have a Mentor Coordinator and you don’t have mentors, talk to your BNI Director. Listen to this podcast, shut it off, pick up the phone, call your local BNI Director and ask them what does it take to get a Mentor Coordinator and have mentors here in your chapter?

We can lay the ground work on how to do the mentors, but then once the mentors start, they need to know what to do. I love the way- It was your idea to put notes in here. There are notes throughout the book because it enables people to say this is an important topic. Focus on this when I am helping somebody. Focus on this.

I view this book as an absolute must-read for everyone who wants to mentor somebody in BNI. Would you not agree?

Carlos:
Completely. You know, you and I kind of formulated it that way. It’s actionable steps that people can take. To be able to sit down with a guy or gal that you are mentoring and say, “Okay. Let’s review what you did in this exercise.” I mean, that is hugely powerful.

Ivan:
Yeah. And you are using examples. You are using content of BNI. You are talking about our code of ethics. You are talking about the VCP process, my VCP process. So there is a lot of content about BNI that I think anybody who really wants to shine and be a great mentor- I think this book helps them do that.

So you did this because you felt that BNI had changed your life in some way. Do you want to- gosh. Time just goes so fast. We are almost out of time, but talk to me about how BNI had changed your life.

Carlos:
No problem. Again, I was the kicking and screaming guy who didn’t want to do it. When I finally went and I realized, holy cow, I could have fun with this. I ended up being Education Coordinator and then I ended up being President after that that- all willingly and not kicking and screaming anymore. I was flattered that people thought that I knew what I was doing.

You know how it goes. You meet one person and then another person. We formed a master mind group out of our BNI group and those core people are the ones who said, “Hey, you have a gift. You need to be a writer.” And two years later, I am doing this full time, making good money, and I still keep in contact with those people. That’s where it all started- at that first meeting at a restaurant going to a BNI meeting.

Ivan:
Did you pattern this book, at a least a little bit, out of your own personal experience because you know, the main character in this book definitely did not like networking.

Carlos:
I would say there is a heavy influence of my life in the book. The main character’s name may be Ken, but the story is very, very similar including, and I won’t give it away but the little part in there about the naysayer. That happened to me, so it is very close. It is very close to what I went through when I joined BNI.

Ivan:
I loved your concept. Everyone, just full disclosure. This is Carlos’ concept 100%. I contributed to this book, but Carlos, you did an amazing job. I am going to, at the conference that I just mentioned at the beginning of this podcast, tell every Director, “ Use this as a manual for teaching people how to be effective mentors in BNI.

I think if our chapters used this content, we would have much, much better mentors, and of course, much better members of the organization.

So where can people get this book, Carlos? It is available at Amazon.com, yes?

Carlos:
You can go on Amazon and just search for “I Love Networking” or go to TheMentorCode.com and the book is on there as well.

Ivan:
I will put a link on here. If you want to go to Amazon, you can pick up the book from Amazon. I think it is out on Kindle Version as well, isn’t it?

Carlos:
So you can either get the paperback version or the ebook version.

Ivan:
Great. And it is also available at BNI.com. Your local Directors may also have it. Feel free to pick it up any time right directly via Kindle or Amazon’s.

Carlos, thank you so much for doing this. I guarantee you, you have made a big different in a lot of people’s lives because you have given the blueprint for people to give back in the organization and they will do that based on the material that you brought to me a few years ago, and I am so glad that I agreed to do this project because it is a great book. I want to thank you for bringing it to me.

Carlos:
Well, thank you. Thank you for being a part of it. Thank you for building BNI because that where, to me, it all started.

Ivan:
Thanks, Carlos. I appreciate it. TheMentorCode.com. The book is I Love Networking. It is said tongue in cheek right now because Ken does not love networking, but he ends of loving it in the end. Oh no, I just gave up the end of the story.

Everybody, thank you very much for listening. Carlos, thank you. Priscilla, back to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Well, that is it for this week, and I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you NetworkingNow.com, the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. . Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Both new and experienced BNI members can benefit from reading this short guide to success in networking. Find your inner networker and learn to love networking!

title.rendered: Episode 379: Being in Sync with Your Networking Partners (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 172.

Synopsis

When Dr. Misner and his wife Beth were on safari in South Africa, there was a suspension bridge between the main lodge and the guest suites. When they didn’t walk in sync, the flexing of the bridge jarred them, but if they walked in sync, with the same stride, it was much easier to cross.

The same is true of networking. You need to walk in sync with your referral partners. You need to cooperate and to collaborate, and that requires two things:

  • Proximity: you have to stay in contact with each other. Have one-to-one meetings.
  • Stay focused on how you can help each other in business. Keep looking for things you can do for your networking partners.

If you do these things, you’ll build deep relationships and trust, and everyone’s business will benefit.

Brought to you by Ask Ivan Misner.

Complete Transcription of BNI Podcast Episode 172 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by AskIvanMisner.com, which is a Web site where you can ask Ivan any question you have about networking.

I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and the chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi, Priscilla. I am doing great, and today I’m actually at the University of La Verne. I sit on their Board of Trustees. And one of the things I talk about in networking is the importance of diversifying your networks, and one type of network that I recommend that people participate in are community service organizations, service clubs like Rotary, Lions, Kiwanis, non-profit organizations. And this is one of those kinds of organizations that I participate in that I think fits that mold.

It’s a private university in Southern California, University of La Verne, and I’ve gotten to be very good friends with the president of the university. And I’ve been sitting on their Board of Trustees now for a year or so. It’s a great way to meet other business people. Unfortunately, the president of the university is stepping down after 25 years as president. That’s really rare. And so I’m on their selection committee to select their new president. And this is a university that I’ve talked about in some of my blogs and how their starting to teach – they have a really good dean in business, and they’re starting to teach more social capital and emotional intelligence.

That’s where I’m at today, but today I want to talk about something that happened on my recent trip to South Africa.

Priscilla:
Oh, great!

Ivan:
And the title of this podcast is Being in Sync With Your Networking Partners. And I’ll tell you how we came up with this. My wife, Beth, and I, we were in South Africa recently on a safari at Camp Jabulani, which is in the Kapama Reserve – and by the way, unbelievable place. Their favorite words are, “Whatever you want.” You’ve got to love someplace like that.

You know, “What time is dinner?” “Whenever you want.”

“When are we going to go out on safari?” “Whenever you want.”

I mean, it was just an amazing – “What’s for dinner?” “Whatever you want.” It was just an amazing place to go. We really enjoyed it.

And they have a beautiful suspension bridge between the main lodge and the guest suites. And if you’ve ever walked over a suspension bridge, you know that the feeling of the springiness, especially if it’s a long suspension bridge, the feeling of the springiness under your feet as you walk. It’s almost like wearing those moon boots that I got for Christmas as a boy many years ago, very bouncy. And I’m talking about a walking suspension bridge, obviously, not a driving one.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
So as we made our way across that suspension bridge, we noticed really quickly that when we didn’t walk in sync, the bridge’s flexing and bending kind of jarred us both. As we tried to walk across it, we’d bounce, it was uncomfortable. But then if we walked in sync with each other in the same stride, it was really easier to walk in a way that didn’t make us look like drunken sailors as we crossed the bridge.

It got me thinking about networking relationships and the importance of being in sync with our referral and our networking partners. When I think about walking in sync with other business people, I think of collaboration, cooperation, and maintaining a focus on how we can help each other, what we call Givers Gain in BNI. And I think these are key elements for being successful in relationship marketing.

And this idea of talking about collaboration, cooperation, and mentioning the University of La Verne at the beginning, do a little side story here, as I go around the world and I talk about networking, especially if I’m in other countries, did a lot of travel over the summer throughout Europe and Africa, inevitably I get somebody who says, “Oh, this BNI thing is so American. It’s so American.”

And I look at them and I say, “It’s the least American thing I’ve ever done in my life.”

And they say, “What do you mean? How can that be?”

And I say, “When you think of American business and you think of corporate America, do you think of cooperation, collaboration, working with each other, helping one another?”

They go, “No.”

I go, “That’s my point.”

Because what I talk about when I teach is all about collaboration and about being in sync. We don’t teach that in colleges and universities throughout the world. We don’t teach that kind of stuff. I believe in order to build the kinds of mutually beneficial relationships that you desire in business, it’s really important to keep in mind a number of things relating to collaboration.

One is what is called the Proximity Effect. The Proximity Effect is – basically, networking is a contact sport, you’ve got to stay in contact with each other, you have to be in touch with one another. There have been a lot of studies about proximity and success and particularly relationships and studies that – one of my favorites was a study that was done in a college, and they said, “Well, who are your best friends,” and they named people. And they said, “Well, why are they your best friends,” and they said, “Well, because we have really close – we do this together, we’re close in this, we think alike,” or whatever, they had all these reasons. “Well, how did you meet?” “Well, he’s my roommate,” or “We’re in two or three classes together.”

And you cannot overlook the power of proximity, and so proximity with purpose, and that purpose being collaboration, can be very, very, very powerful. So staying in touch, holding one-to-ones with each other to learn more about one another’s business, asking each other how can you help, having that Givers Gain focus is really an effective way of staying in sync and supporting your referral partners.

We all need to make a commitment to maintain focus on how we can help each other in business. This is a kind of a new thought pattern, I think, for most business owners. We’re not schooled or trained in thinking how can I help you, and when it comes to those with whom we have this particular business community, it’s even more important than ever to maintain those kinds of relationships, too. Look for ways to help each other and to be in sync with one another, because I really, truly think – I know we’re running out of time – that relationship networking is all about collaboration and cooperation and being in sync.

And so in BNI, the more you can find ways to help and support each other in business and in your lives, the more you’re going to create friendships and relationships that will turn into genuine business.

Priscilla:
Great! I just want to add one thing, which I think is having the one-on-ones and really getting to know your fellow members is really the best way to be in sync, because then when something comes up, you really can identify who to match them with, and it’s – because you know those people, and exactly what you’re talking about.

Ivan:
Yep, the one-to-ones are really important. I learned something in Europe, that the phrase “one-on-one,” though, Priscilla, that you should be aware of –

Priscilla:
Did I say, “one-on-one”?

Ivan:
One-on-one has a completely connotation, so you get members going, “Yeah, I want a one-on-one,” and you get into a lot of trouble. So what we try to use now the phrase, “one-to-one” to keep ourselves out of court.

Priscilla:
Okay, good.

Ivan:
Which, I think, is a funny story. It’s amazing to me how things are so – we do the same program all around the world, but there are those little differences like – I think I may have mentioned this in a previous podcast – in Sweden, they don’t have the expression “word of mouth.” Did you know that?

Priscilla:
No.

Ivan:
No expression, so you know how they translate “word of mouth”?

Priscilla:
How?

Ivan:“Mouth to mouth.”

Priscilla:
That’s funny! Yeah.

Ivan:
We’ve explained that that, too, has a completely different connotation in the United States.

Priscilla:
That wouldn’t work here!

Ivan:
Well, so just to wrap up with this piece, I think being in sync is all about collaboration and proximity, got to stay in touch, got to be connected, got to do the one-to-ones, and find ways to help each other. And that’s the way you build relationships and generate business.

Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, you’re welcome. Thank you, Dr. Misner.

Well, that’s it for this week. I’d just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by AskIvanMisner.com. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you’ll join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

When they didn’t walk in sync, the flexing of the bridge jarred them, but if they walked in sync, with the […]

title.rendered: Episode 378: Two Tips for Moving People in the Right Direction

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Any time you hold a leadership position, you face the challenge of getting people to follow the direction you think they need to go. It’s important not to burn down the barn in order to roast the pig. There are two sayings that Dr. Misner shares with aspiring leaders, in or out of BNI.

  1. People don’t care how much you know until they know how much you care. Unsolicited advice is never acceptable. If you are new to a leadership position, talk to the members of a group before making sweeping changes.
  2. Diplomacy is the art of letting someone else have your way. You need to learn how to work with people rather than through people. Note that this saying is about collaboration, not manipulation.

These two sayings between them form the cornerstone to great leadership.

Share your own techniques for successful leadership, or occasions when you’ve used these.

Brought to you by The Global Networking Show.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 378 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by the NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloyou by the GlobalNetworkingShow.com, a montly Google Hangout show, hosted by Andy Lopata and Dr. Ivan Misner. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi Priscilla. I just got back from New Orleans for the US Member Extravaganza trip that we had. Everyone had a great time. We had hundreds of people come, mostly winners of the membership drives in the US. A good time was had by all.

Priscilla:
Good. Did you eat some great food?

Ivan:
There is always great food in New Orleans. Always.

So today I am talking about two tips for moving people in the right direction. Let me see if I can give you a sense about what this is about. There are always people who are experts and have knowledge and information. The challenge is trying to get people to listen to that knowledge and information. This is particularly true- if you have ever had a leadership team role in any organization or in BNI, if you are on the leadership team, are an educational coordinator or whatever, whatever role of leadership that you have, but frankly, it could apply anywhere.

You know, it’s getting people to go in one direction, particularly with business professionals, entrepreneurs, and salespeople, it is more like herding cats. It’s not very easy to get people to follow in the direction that you think they need to go.

So I had a person who is a leader in BNI who went to one of the groups and was really appalled by how bad things were being run by the members. She let them know in no uncertain terms what they were doing wrong and what they needed to do to turn it around. What I found interesting later- I wasn’t there but heard about it- was that her assessment of the situation and the solutions that she proposed were spot on. Absolutely correct. But her presentation of the solution was dead wrong.

She was so blunt with the group members that she received a really negative reaction from them, leaving the place in a bigger mess than it was when she first walked in. When I met her and talked to her about what she might have done differently, she was furious with me for not supporting her since she was right and the members of the group were absolutely wrong.

I kept talking to her about the fact that I wasn’t arguing that she was right because she was. The problem that I had with how she handled the situation in that area- it was the way she handled the situation. It wasn’t whether she was right of wrong, because she was right, but how she handled it. I tried to explain. I shared one of my favorite sayings for a dilemma like this, which is “don’t burn down the barn to roast the pig”.

If you are doing something, don’t make it worse than what you started with. She could never really wrap her head around the concept that people may not welcome her advice with the enthusiasm that fit it and she expected.

So I ask people to do two things that are really important before you can get people to follow you. I think they are key in being a good leader. Whether you are on the leadership team for BNI, an education coordinator, membership committee- whatever your title. Even talking about BNI or some other organization. These are two things that kind of set the mindset for how you should work with people. Okay, ready?

Priscilla:
I’m ready.

Ivan:
The first one is people don’t care how much you know until they know how much you care.

Priscilla:
I have heard that.

Ivan:
That is so important. If you want people to listen to you when you are coaching them or redirecting them, they have to know that you care about their success and that you want them to succeed. If they don’t know down to their core, they will not listen to your advice. Ever. Period. Full stop.

Oftentimes, that means do not offer advice the first time up. Unsolicited advice is never acceptable. People don’t like it, so don’t offer unsolicited advice, but there are ways to get people to ask for advice. Whatever you do, I would recommend- like if you are taking over a new leadership role, let’s say you become a chapter president or one of the other senior positions. I would not recommend coming in and making changes overnight.

Talk to the participants. Find out where they think the group should go and the kinds of things that they have in mind before you start ruffling feathers. People don’t care how much you know until they know how much you care. That is really important.

The second thing is a saying that was given to me by my mother on a paperweight. I think I have talked about this is a couple of other podcasts. I have the paperweight right here, right here in front of me. My mom gave it to me when I was 16years old. I was running an uphill battle in a student council race. My mother gave me this paperweight and the paperweight says that “Diplomacy is the art of letting someone else have your way.”

Diplomacy is the art of letting someone else have your way. When she gave that to me, she explained that I had to learn how to work with people, not through people. She said that even if I did know the answer to a problem, it did no good if no one else believed me. That advice helped me win the election and it helped me many times through my life.

I admit I don’t always use it as well as I can. However, I do use it and things go much more smoothly when I do. It is real important to note that this concept that diplomacy is the art of letting someone else have your way is about collaboration, not manipulation. That is really important. It’s about collaboration, not manipulation.

So the more you are able to collaborate with people to get input, to get their suggestions- not all suggestions are necessarily good. I get that. But the more that you can get engagement from participants, I think the better a leader you will be.

So two simple things to help move people in the right direction is that you have to show them that you care. You have to show them, not just tell them that you care. There is a book by a good friend of mine, Stephen M.R. Covey called The Speed of Trust. He has a quote in there and I am trying to remember it right off the top of my head. I think it is relevant here. You can’t talk your way out of a problem that you behaved your way into.

You can’t talk your way out of a problem that you behaved your way into. So it is not just talking about the fact that you care. Your behavior has to show that you care about their success. Then the second part is diplomacy. You have to learn how to collaborate, not manipulate but collaborate with people in order to nudge them in the right direction.

If you do those two things, and you have, I think, the cornerstone for great leadership.

Priscilla:
Great. The one thing that came to mind when you were talking about the first tip, the way to keep people from being defensive if they really believe that you care about them. You are not just bossing them around or criticizing them but that you actually care for them to do better at whatever it is that you are discussing.

Ivan:
Right. You are absolutely right. There are a lot of techniques that you can use to show you care about issues. There is a really good technique by Dr. Mark McKergow. There is a book called Solutions Focus, which is a great book which talks about, you know, focusing on solutions. He has a technique that he uses. On a scale from one to ten, how bad is the problem? And then, talk through the problem when people give that score. It is a way of honing in on the key elements of the challenge and, more importantly, what is the solution to the challenge? I think that is a really good one.

And then there is an old technique that has been around forever, the “feel-so-found”. I understand the way that you feel. So and so felt the same way until they found out- that is a great technique that shows empathy. I know where you are coming from. I get that. I know a group of some people who had a similar situation. Here is how they resolved it. You don’t make it personal, but you focus on the solutions.

Those are some simple techniques to apply to the people don’t care how much you idea.

Priscilla:
Great.

Ivan:
Well, that is all I have got for today. I would love to hear from you, the listeners, if you have some other techniques or if you have used any of these in BNI or outside of BNI. How did it fare for you? And if you have some other techniques, please share them here.

Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s great. Well, I think that’s it for this week, and I want to thank you, Dr. Misner. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the GolbalNetworkingShow.com, hosted by Andy Lopata and our very own Dr. Ivan Misner. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

It’s important not to burn down the barn in order to roast the pig. There are two sayings that Dr. Misner shares with aspiring leaders, in or out of BNI. People […]

title.rendered: Episode 377: Your Contribution Lives On

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Priscilla recommended this week’s topic based on Dr. Misner’s blog post reflecting on the death of Robin Williams.

Albert Pine, an English author who wrote in the early 19th century, said,

“What we do for ourselves dies with us. What we do for others and the world remains unchanged and is immortal.”

The Givers Gain philosophy is one that encourages us to do for others. Whether what we give lives beyond us or just makes people happier while we’re here, it’s a good thing. Each of us makes a difference to the people whose lives we touch.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 377 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by the NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi Priscilla. I am actually at headquarters this week. I am not traveling and visiting regions but working at headquarters.

Priscilla:
Oh great.

Ivan:
Every now and then I have to show up.

Priscilla:
They need you there.

Ivan:
Yes. Hey, so today’s topic is an interesting topic. I’ll say I had not planned on doing this one, but you recommended that I do it. So I am doing it based on your recommendation.

Priscilla:
I’m glad because I think it’s a great topic.

Ivan:
I did a blog and it is this blog that Priscilla read. She said that she thought it would make a great podcast topic. I had not planned on doing this because it is kind of personal. It is indirectly related to BNI, and that is the news of Robin Williams’ suicide last month.

You know, he was someone who many people globally felt strongly about. You almost felt like you knew him. He was in so many movies and on television, and you know, you just have to wonder about the situation that led to him taking his own life.

I have a really good friend, Yakov Smirnoff, who is a comedian. He and I just spoke recently. Yakov did a PBS special talking about Robin Williams’ life that aired in September. I recommend that you go watch that if you can. He did a great special.

I think it was that when I found out that Robin Williams took his life, it really led me to some deeper and more profound thinking. There is a quote by Albert Pine, an English author, who wrote in the early 19th century. He said, “What we do for ourselves dies with us. What we do for others and the world remains unchanged and is immortal.”

There is no doubt that Robin Williams has left a mark on our world. I spent hours laughing through several of his movies, and of course, his comedy shows. But, I think to paraphrase Pine, I would say that what we do for ourselves ends with us. What we do for others lives on.

I think in many ways, BNI does that for many, many people. I came up with the phrase a few years back “changing the way the world does business”. I think that change comes by implementing a shift in the focus of how we go about growing our businesses. We change, we shift from this sort of dog-eat-dog competitive model to a model of how can I help you? How can I collaborate with you?

This collaborative model is a concept that is definitely changing the way that the world does business. One of our BNI directors recently said to me, “I know that our mission is all about referrals. We know referrals are our purpose. But impacting someone’s life is our calling.” I thought that was a very powerful statement. We know referrals are our purpose, but impacting someone’s life is our calling.

When doing business with a givers gain philosophy, I think that can really get imbedded in practice. There is a huge movement from transactional relationships to transformational relationships. Both people and businesses take on fresh dimensions of trust and creativity that can’t be measured with mere numbers. That ethos and experience multiplied in a viral fashion changes the face of business, which in turn, changes lives in a positive way.

Douglas Adams, author of the Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Universe wrote that anything that is in the world when you are born is normal and ordinary and is just a natural part of the way the world works. Anything that is invented between when you are about 15 and 35 is new, exciting and revolutionary. And you can probably get a career in it. This givers gain business focus started when I was 28 years old, and it has provided me with a rewarding and long career.

I think if we can all use our loss of one of the world’s great comedians and actors to start a conversation about what our contribution that is going to be that will live on past our life span, I think that would be a powerful conversation. It could be a powerful conversation even at a BNI meeting. Possibly, one week instead of just 60 second introductions, what you hope to contribute through the work that you are doing, the work that you are doing in your BNI chapter, the profession that you have.

No matter what you do, I would encourage listeners to design a life with that in mind. I have a good friend, Stuart Emory, who I wrote Who’s in Your Room? with. He has a great concept and is actually working on another book with it. It’s whatever you are, be a good one. It doesn’t matter what it is- whatever it is that you are, be a good one. That was pretty much in a nutshell the blog I wrote, Priscilla.

I would like to hear from you why you think that my observations about Robin would be relevant to the BNI members. I would love to hear that.

Priscilla:
Well, I think it is very touching, and I think that it is important, what we do in the world. It is something that I think about a lot. It is hard to understand if you are just a person just doing your work, even if you are a good person and you are doing good work, how that is going to make a difference in the world beyond your lifetime. You know, I am sure that a lot of us feel that way because not many of us are famous or extremely wealthy.

And so, it is something that I think about a lot. When you linked it to givers gain, I really think that what is unique about your organization and BNI in general is givers gain, what’s so special about it. I just thought this is something that we can do on an ongoing basis, person to person, and maybe it will live beyond us but maybe it will just make people happier while we are here. Either way, it’s a good thing.

Ivan:
Yeah, either way, it’s a good thing. I have said for years that you may not be able to make a world of difference, but you can make a difference in the world. Applying that givers gain philosophy is absolutely making a difference in the world and making a difference to that one person.

There is an old story that has been around for years and years and years. You may have heard it. It goes something like this: a man was walking along the beach and he saw some guy walking along the beach in front of him. There had been some big tide that had come in and washed some starfish up on the beach. I mean, hundreds and hundreds and thousands of starfish were laying along the beach and this man was just kind of walking along the beach and bending over and picking up and throwing starfish back into the ocean one at a time.

You know, this went on for like a mile. There were thousands of these and he certainly couldn’t save them all. The guy walked up to him and said, “What are you doing?”

He said, “I am throwing the starfish back in.”

He said, “Yeah, but you could never make a difference here. There are just thousands and thousands of them.”

And he picked up a starfish, looked at it and threw it in and said, “It made a difference to that one.”

Priscilla:
Right. Yeah.

Ivan:
That is really what I think I am talking about and what you are talking about with givers gain. It is making a difference to that one. It is making a difference to other people, to individuals. We can all do that. In many ways, you, the listener, the BNI member, make a bigger difference than I can in that you are working week in and week out with people helping them build their business. You are building long term relationships with the other people around that table, around those tables, and you are making a big difference for a handful of people. That is real.

I think kind of understanding what your mission is, what you want to accomplish, and what kind of difference you can make is important for everyone to do. I wish Robin Williams had done that. I wish other people I know who have gone out the same way that Robin has had thought about that because we all have the ability to make a difference in people’s lives.

That’s all today, Priscilla. I hope that was what you were looking for. It was an unusual topic for us, but I was happy to do it.

Priscilla:
I think it was a great topic, and I really value what you said. And I think that the listeners will like it, too.

Ivan:
Well, we will find out. If you could drop me a comment here- and feel free to say, “What in the world are you thinking with this as a podcast?” or whatever you would like. I read all of the posts on the podcast. Thank you very much.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Well, I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard
