title.rendered: Episode 358: Who’s in Your Room?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Stewart Emery joins Dr. Misner to talk about his new book, Who’s in Your Room?, which was the theme for International Networking Week 2014.

Imagine that you live in just one room. With you in this room are all the people in your life. In this room there is just one door, and the door operates just one way: anyone and anything that enters cannot leave. Ever.

As far as your mind is concerned, this is true: nothing we have let in can be completely removed.

With regard to BNI, if we let in members who are not good for the group, getting rid of them is a painful and difficult experience for the group.

Look for people who are a match for your values before you invite them in. Establish a mental “doorman” to ensure that only the right people and projects get through. In BNI, the membership committee acts as the doorman.

The book is available as a special BNI edition.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 358 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi Priscilla. This week, I am in Paris. It is the week of my 25th wedding anniversary. I promised my lovely bride that I would take her to Paris for our 25th, and that is where we are this week.

Priscilla:
Congratulations.

Ivan:
Thanks. I also have a guest with me today. He is a very good friend, Stewart Emery. He was the first CEO of what is now Landmark Education. He has authored numerous bestselling books including Do You Matter- How Great Design Will Make People Love Your Company. He helped Barnes and Noble to develop the Nook. He has written a new book which he was generous enough to invite me to participate with called Who’s in Your Room? Which is our topic today. Stewart, welcome to the podcast.

Stewart:
I am happy to be here. Thanks for the invitation, Ivan.

Ivan:
I want to ask you a question or two about the book, but before I do, I sincerely want to thank you for inviting me to participate in this book. I first heard about this concept several years ago. Stewart and I, for anyone listening, are in an organization called the Transformational Leadership Council.

I heard Stewart do a presentation one day at the Transformational Leadership Council, and in a European term, I was godsmacked. I was just completely blown away by the concept. I thought oh my goodness, this fits BNI so well. I nudged Stewart for a couple of years to do this book, and you were generous enough to invite me to help you with it, Stewart. I really do appreciate that.

Can you give everyone sort of a Reader’s Digest version of the concept of Who’s in Your Room?

Stewart:
Absolutely. All of you listening, I want you to turn on your imaginations. I want you to imagine that you live in just one room, and I want you to imagine that you spend your entire life in this room. What is in this room is all the people in your life, as well as all the projects and the relationships and obligations that come along with them.

But I want you to imagine that this room only has one door and this door only operates one way: in, not out. There is not exit. Now, imagine that whoever comes through this door and whatever they bring with them cannot leave. Ever. They will remain in your room with you forever.

So who is in your room? It is a simple enough question. For one, it has a profound effect on the lives of the people who have tried to answer this question. In the book we have stories of people who have done this and how this changes their lives for the better and how you put into practice all the ideas of the book.

Just before your mind starts saying to you, oh but Stewart, you can’t be serious. It can’t literally be proved that whoever comes into our room is with us forever. Actually, as far as your mind is concerned, it is literally proven. Anything that comes into the experience of your existence gets stored somewhere in your little grey cells and often it will come back to haunt you, sometimes without you even knowing that.

The book is about, okay, how do you handle what you don’t want in your room anymore that can’t actually be in exodus, and then how do you use this idea to build the life of your dreams? That is what the book is about.

Ivan:
It’s true. This fits what we do in BNI so well because we literally let people, not figuratively and not metaphorically, we literally let people into our room in BNI. If we are not selective about what we look for in terms of values and where the person is coming from in their ethics, then we are completely letting the wrong people into the room, and getting them out of the room in BNI is really, really difficult sometimes.

In our concept of people in your room forever, how do you cut their influence in your life in particular when you have them in your room?

Stewart:
The people who you are in your room that you are not having an active relationship with, that could be an ex-husband, (ex-) wife, (ex-) boyfriend , (ex-)girlfriend, boss or whoever. While they are not literally in your room, the way that you store their memory and deal with them is there.

Unfortunately, the human mind is much better at creating new memories and new structures than getting rid of old ones. So the strategy is that you pay attention to what you actually want in your life and you look for people who are (of value, who support your passion) and are interested in what you are interested in, people that you can learn from. You find ways to invite them into your room and be invited into their room.

If you do this, with focus and determination and good intention, what happens to the stuff in your room- it progressively has less and less and less influence. So the memories are there in the distant past, but they are not active enough to contaminate your life on a daily basis. That is basically the strategy.

Ivan:
But how can you build what you want, not just the things that you don’t want. You know, a lot of people will look at this and go, I don’t this kind of person and I don’t want that kind of person. What are the kinds of things specifically to get the kind of person that you do want in your room?

Stewart:
So let’s make this BNI specific. You have a membership committee and I know you have a list of the attributes of the kind of people that you want to be in a BNI chapter. You know, you might have “plays well with others”. You might have “ego in check”. You might have “a giver not a taker”. We could go down the list and call those the operating principles or the core values of BNI.

A particular chapter in a particular area may have demographic and geographic values added to that list. Then when you are looking for new members, in addition to the value that you don’t have multiple representatives of a single profession in a chapter, you have this list of values and the selection committee and the membership will have a chance to determine whether there is a possible new member. In fact, all of those values represent the things that matter to us.

On that basis, you invite them in, and if they don’t on that basis, you don’t invite them in. No matter how compelling it might be that they will be good for business, they just won’t be.

There is a saying that a good deal with a bad person is a bad deal. A bad deal with a good person, well you can work it out. I know you know that, Ivan. We have both been around the block enough to know that to be a living truth for us.

Ivan:
Listen, the first time I heard you do this presentation, I came home and let go of somebody who I had been working with on a project for a long time. He came to the project with so much drama. He had great skills but he brought so much drama to the project that I just had to cut him loose. It was a direct result of hearing you talk about these very kinds of things.

In the book, we talk about a doorman. Do you want to explain the doorman, briefly if you can, because that is a lot of-

Stewart:
Oh, absolutely. In your case the doorman is the membership committee. So imagine- I suppose that may be for both of us Ivan and really young people may not remember, but once upon a time, good hotels had doormen, and good hotels still do. The doorman decided whether you were going to get in or you weren’t.

So in this who’s in your room, which is an imaginary virtual reality here, we want you to imagine that you are going to hire a virtual doorman. This doorman, you have to train. The doorman has to learn what it is that you are passionate about, what it is that matters to you. What your goals and dreams are, what your values are.

The doorman then has put on- I don’t suppose he has to be a bouncer. I suppose more he has to be a kung fu expert. This doorman has to be put on duty and the doorman doesn’t let anybody through the door of your room who doesn’t have shared values with you and who doesn’t support what matters to you, doesn’t support your dream, doesn’t support your passion.

The doorman also comes in as a talent scout. He might say this person will really help Ivan establish a new chapter of BNI in XYZ country. Or the doorman might say Sallie has just come into BNI and I know that she would love to bring some quality people along with her. I think this person would be great for her and great for BNI.

So the doorman is making a choice. It’s all virtual, of course.

Ivan:
That is the point there. This is your inner mind working as the screening element of who you are going to invite into your life, or for us, invite into BNI.

Stewart:
Correct. The thing of it, Ivan, is that you mind is always doing this for the most part without your awareness.

Ivan:
Right. But you are not consciously making decisions based on the values unless you really sit down and think of them.

Stewart:
Correct. I noticed that our community, where is the book is out and people will be looking at copies, you know there is a new kind of mean. The mean is “in my room” or “not in my room”.

Some may say, “Stewart, do you know so and so? Are they in your room?” I may say, “No. I don’t know them.” And they say, “Maybe you would like them in your room.” So it has become like a shorthand. I think that is a great thing. Like “things go better with Coke”- “Who’s in your room?” One of those means.

Ivan:
Stewart, I am afraid that we are out of time. For the members of BNI, this book is now available. We have it out as a BNI edition. There is a BNI edition of it. It was our goal, Stewart, and Rick and I, to have the public edition out later this year, but you can get the BNI edition now. It is on BNI.com. It is not going to be available at Amazoin, so go to BNI.com to get it.

Stewart, thank you so much for including me in this book. It was very kind of you. I love the concept. I love the material. I am sure that we are going to be talking about this for a long time.

Stewart:
Ivan, I am so glad that you are in my room.

Ivan:
Thank you, Stewart. I am very privileged to be in your room. I appreciate it a great deal. Thanks for being on the podcast today. Priscilla, back to you.

Priscilla:
Well, I think that is it for this week. Thank you so much, Dr. Misner. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thanks for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Stewart Emery joins Dr. Misner to talk about his new book, Who’s in Your Room? Dr. Misner is a contributor to the book, which is available in a special BNI edition.

title.rendered: Episode 357: The Secret to Great Leadership Teams

content.rendered:
March 2020 Update: The original BNI Fast Track CDs are no longer available, but the Maximize BNI Package over at www.misneraudio.com includes BNI Networking Secrets, The BNI Visitor Experience, and The Secret to Great Leadership Teams.

Synopsis

Sara Minnis and Flynn Nogueira from Del Fuego join Dr. Misner to talk about the newest CD in the BNI Fast Track Program, “The Secret to Great Leadership Teams.”

Here are some key points from the new series.

  • The speed and tone of the leadership team affect the speed and tone of the chapter.
  • Accountability is the key to successful chapters.
  • Leadership teams are the key to accountability.

People who make good leadership team members make good leaders in general. Serving on your BNI chapter’s leadership team develops your abilities as a leader–and it will also bring you more business, because it increases your professional credibility.

Visit BNI.delfuego.com to pre-order the new “Secret to Great Leadership Teams” CDs or buy the original Fast Track program.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 357 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan. How are you doing today and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi Priscilla. I am doing great, and I have just been to Dusseldorf for BNI Germany. I am on my way to France, so I will be in Europe for several weeks.

Priscilla:
Fun. Who do you have with you today?

Ivan:
I have two guests today. I have the Del Fuego ladies, Sara Minnis and Flynn Nogueira. Many of you who have listened to my podcast before know that Del Fuego has been a strategic partner with BNI for 10 years. We have been actively working with chapters and Del Fuego in what is called the Fast Track Program. Ladies, I would love to hear an update on that in a minute.

We have been doing Fast Track for about six years. Flynn and Sarah- we call them the Del Fuego sisters- are here today to talk about Fast Track and some new stuff. We got in studio recently and put together a couple of new sets and we are going to talk about one of them today.

Ladies, how are you. Update me on how Fast Track is doing. Many of the members may know about it. Some may not.

Sara:
Fast Track is doing great. Hello, everyone. This is Sara. I will give you a quick update and then Flynn can give you a little update. We have more chapters jumping on Fast Track. For those of you who don’t know about it, you can just Google “Fast Track”. You will find out a lot about it.

It’s a chapter development program that really trains the entire chapter in BNI fundamentals as well as great inviting habits now that we have put it into a monthly fee jumping on. We see consistently the same results from the minimum increase to the maximum increase in booked revenue anywhere from 20% to – one member told me at the BNI conference 300%. Chapters grow.

In Fast Track, we uplift it about every six months, change out the materials and keep it fresh, but it’s still really, really fun to support. It’s growing.

Flynn?

Flynn:
Ivan, one of the things that we have really learned over the years that I think just as we started the subscription program has come to light more. That is how critical leadership teams are to the chapter, which is why we ask you to do the CD that we are going to be releasing very, very soon on leadership teams. What we would like to do is we just have a few points. We thought it would be so fun to give you some highlights that we talk about on the CDs. Is that good with you?

Ivan:
Yeah, I would love to. Before we do, let’s mention the domain where people can get information on Fast Track. I believe it is BNI.delfuego.com. Is that correct?

Flynn and Sara:
That is correct.

Ivan:
So just go there if you want more information on Fast Track. Alright, ladies. Hit me with it.

Sara:
The first one that you talk about is speed and tone of the leadership team and how that affects the speed and tone of the chapter. What is that all about?

Ivan:
Listen, the speed of the leadership team determines the speed of the chapter. It’s that way in any business. It is that way in any organization. I see chapters that just rock and they almost always have a leadership team that is really on top of it, highly engaged. They are, in fact, leaders.

And you know, chapters that may be struggling sometimes have leaders or a leadership team that is completely on board. Now sometimes, the chapter has struggled and they replace the leadership team that now is on top of things and you start to see things turn around.

The speed and tone of the leadership team, absolutely sets the tone of the chapter. Always has and probably always will.

Flynn:
You know, that is so true because I would say of the close to 1000 chapters that have done Fast Track now, we see that consistently. That is the case.

Here is the second one: accountability is the key to successful chapters. What is that all about?

Ivan:
Well, we talk at length about accountability and the truth is that accountability is the key to any success. Full stop, end of statement.

Accountability is important and focusing on those metrics is what makes a chapter successful. Predictors of success include looking at the metrics of a chapter. It is one of the reasons why we try to put as much into BNI Connect, our online program, as possible. How is the attendance? How many referrals are being passed? What is the thank you for closed business?

Then, focusing on that accountability with the leadership team is, I think, really key. That means you have to have discussions about how people are doing. They need to be discussions that are positive and supportive.

Sara:
One last one that we want to throw out there that you talk about that is so wonderful on the CDs- and that is the leadership teams are the keys to accountability. Tell us a little bit more about that.

Ivan:
Let’s talk about this one for a while. They are the keys to accountability and the way they do it can make a big difference in the culture of the chapter- so that when leadership teams say things like, “You are not doing what you promised to do,” or “You need to do better,” and they are being very punitive, it makes people defensive.

Instead, leadership teams and membership committees, the best approach to maintain accountability and do it in a positive way is to sit down and say, “Okay. How can we help you __________?” and fill in the blank.

How can we help you bring more referrals? How can we help you bring visitors? How can we help you increase the quality of your referrals? Whatever the issue is, how can we help you? Do it from a perspective of caring rather than being punitive.

I am a real believer in following the policies and the system of our program. There has got to be rules. You can’t run a sports match without having rules. You can’t run a network effectively without having rules and unless you just want to make it a social club. There has to be rules.

And you don’t want to be a fascist applying them, either. That is what happens. Also what happens is sometimes people think- you know what? Sometimes they don’t think. They just react. They do.

They just react. They have somebody not doing their job and they just react. They are frustrated. You can always tell when people are frustrated because they may say things in jest. They are trying to be cutsie when in fact they are trying to be slightly offensive. That happens when people haven’t really thought through the impact of what they are about to say.

So you have got to hold people accountable but you have to do it in a caring way. Leadership teams who get that down really work.

I think one of the things that we talk about, and we don’t have enough time to go through it all today, is the “can’t do, won’t do” mentality. What we talk about on the CDs is gold and I have trained Directors on for decades on this concept.

If you ask somebody, “How can I help you __________?” and then fill in the blank, almost always they give you an answer that is a “can’t do” answer or a “won’t do” answer. It is going to be one of those two answers.

The can’t do answer is an answer that basically identifies that they have a skill that they are lacking. They are having difficultly and need assistance. If you are talking about networking, you know what? We are obligated to help people who need it in networking. The leadership team, the Director Consultant or Executive Director, the national office, and BNI headquarters- our goal is to help teach people how to network more effectively if they don’t know how. So a “can’t do” – I love “can’t do’s”. I was a “can’t do”. I was. I didn’t know how to network. I had to learn how to do it.

Well, my members don’t have to learn on their own. They can get feedback from people to help them, starting with the leadership teams. So a “can’t do” is struggling.

A “won’t do”, to make a long story short, just won’t do it for whatever reason. They are just not in. They are not committed. It is not the right time. It’s a full moon on a Tuesday. Who knows? They are not going to do it and you treat those “won’t do’s” substantially differently than you treat those “can’t do’s”.

If you can just figure out if you have someone who is a “won’t do” or a “can’t do”, you have already narrowed down the problem substantially. But it all comes don’t to accountability. No organization is successful without some kind of accountability.

You know, one of the strengths of BNI groups is that most of the members are friends. One of the weaknesses of BNI groups is that most of the members are friends. Friends don’t like to hold friends accountable. We just did in a few minutes what we did in the better part of 40 minutes, probably. Or more. I don’t know how long the CDs add up to but substantially more time.

That is the leadership team material that the two of you and I just recently did.

Lynn:
I know that we all did it together, Ivan. These are great CDs. I think there is so much about leadership and not just how to be a great member of a leadership team but you talked so much about the skills that it takes to be a great leader. I think these CDs will really help leadership teams and I think they will help the members in their businesses be better leaders. I really do.

Ivan:
I think you are right and I think people who make a good member of a leadership team also tend to be good leaders in general. The skill set is transferable. It’s one of the reasons why I recommend that people volunteer to be on the leadership team because it does develop your skills in managing and leading.

I think one of the things that people really learn in BNI is the ability to interact effectively with other people in a business context. Of course, that exposure also leads to more business in case after case that I have seen where people have actually generated more business because they were on the leadership team. Being on the leadership team shows people how good they are at what they do, and even if it is in a leadership context, it rubs off on their credibility in business.

Sara:
Absolutely. You know, even the part where you explain the role of each leadership team member and what each role really means is fabulous. I know even if someone listened to just that part, they would get so much more out of not just their role but how all of the roles work together.

Ivan:
I agree. We are just about out of time. Anything you want to add to our podcast today?

Flynn:
I think we should say that people in chapter subscription will receive leadership team CDs for their leadership team as part of the subscription program. Otherwise, they will be at the BNI store in the Leadership Team CD package.

Ivan:
Yes. They will be. You know, it is not just the CDs that you ladies provide. It is the webinars, the telebridges, the coaching that you do that has really made a difference for many of our members worldwide. I appreciate what you ladies do. You have contributed to a lot of lives in this organization and I appreciate it.

Sara:
Thank you, Ivan. We love it. Love it.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That’s great. I think that’s it for this week. I want to thank all of you for that podcast. I would just like to remind the listeners, that this podcast has been brought to you by networkingnow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

The speed and tone of the leadership team affect the speed and tone of the chapter. […]

title.rendered: Episode 356: 5 Tips for Using LinkedIn (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 225.

Synopsis

LinkedIn is the top site for online business networking. There’s an official BNI group there, where members can share ideas with each other.

Here are some ways you can get more out of LinkedIn.

  1. Connect with more people through your second and third level connections.
  2. Participate in groups, which allow you to communicate with people you aren’t directly connected to.
  3. Capitalize on the search engine optimization value of LinkedIn in your profile
  4. Connect LinkedIn with Twitter for cross-promotion.
  5. Build and enhance your credibility with recommendations, publications, and work history.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 225 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing great and I am actually back in town this week. I travel a lot but this week I actually get to be here at BNI headquarters.

Priscilla:
That’s great. What are you sharing with us?

Ivan:
Today I am going to talk about five tips for getting the most out of LinkedIn. I have said for a long time that social media and BNI- it’s not an either or. It’s and. Being active in online networking, there is a lot of value to that. I am a fan. I am also obviously a fan of face to face networking. I talk a lot about face to face networking, so today, I thought I’d talk about online networking, particularly LinkedIn.

If any of you out there have any lingering thoughts that social media is kind of a fad, you want to let those go- especially in light of LinkedIn’s IPO, where they had a valuation of $4.3 billion USD. It’s just amazing. When an IPO says- well, you know, a public offering. So they are here to stay.

I have been following the development of online business networking for some years, particularly the inception of some sites like ecademy.com, which is one of my favorite online sites. It’s based out of Europe. I know the founders there really well. LinkedIn, which I am going to talk about today. Of course, Facebook and Twitter. There area lot of competitors out there to LinkedIn. It’s certainly for now, at the top of the sites devoted primarily to online business networking.

I use a variety of social networks to interact with colleagues and friends, but LinkedIn has some features that I think are really strong. A lot of my members use LinkedIn, so I wanted to talk about that. LinkedIn has an official BNI group. And I think it’s a great way for BNI members to stay in touch. We recently had a call about BNI Connect. Obviously, BNI Connect is a great way for members to stay in touch. LinkedIn is another and I do recommend it. Sometimes a person gets in that 500+ connections category and then they don’t know what to do. They have a lot of connections. Now what do I do?

Here are five tips that I want to give people. One is connecting with more people. I have spoken to countless entrepreneurs who have increased their business because of connections they were able to make on LinkedIn. With the ability to view detailed profiles, which is what we do with BNI Connetc, and the ability meet via a shared acquaintance and then to be able to post updates about one’s business or career- these are things that are really valuable and important. I think it is something that LinkedIn offers that members should take advantage of.

LinkedIn is also known as a source for job seekers and recruiters. I think that’s probably one of its biggest advantages and opportunities. Most of my BNI members have a job, so they are not looking for anything else, but you might have friends or associates who are looking for jobs. Or you might be looking to hire somebody. LinkedIn is well-known as a great website for job seekers. As an employer, that might be of value to you. Or if you have friends who are looking for jobs, clearly that would be of value to you.

So the first is connecting with more people. The second is participating in groups. LinkedIn’s groups features a wonderful way to meet others with a shared affinity, whether it is a cause, an employer, an organization like BNI- BNI has an official group on LinkedIn. Being an official member of the same group as someone else removes the barrier that LinkedIn ordinarily imposes, that you have to personally know someone to send a message or invite them to connect. This gives you a way to communicate with people.

I am on several groups in LinkedIn and I have found it invaluable, particularly as an educational element to talk about different topics. LinedkIn’s group feature might be its most valuable feature, especially when it is used effectively to build connections, participate in a group by asking questions, suggesting topics or recommending another member’s answers is a way to build a more personal connection. I see this as a thing that as BNI Connect grows and grows and grows, we are going to do more of what LinkedIn is doing but within that walled garden where it’s only BNI members mentoring and supporting each other.

Again it’s not either or. Don’t do one and not the other. I think both are great and have opportunities. Something that I recommend is capitalizing on the search engine optimization. LinkedIn profiles show up high in search results. The more links you have, the higher up a profile might show in the search engine results. Obviously, search engine results- if you have a BNI member who is a social media expert, talk to them. BNI allows you to incorporate very important links: to your websites, blog- when you incorporate these things to your profile, it not only builds your profile link, but it adds to your site. I use that feature to highlight my blog, businessnetworking.com. So I do a lot of promotion as well with my blog.

The fourth thing is tying that with a Twitter connection. LinkedIn easily dovetails with Twitter. Indeed, you can really integrate with Twitter and with several social networks using Twitter’s programming social interfaces. I cross promote content that I have written across various social networking accounts. Every article I write can be seamlessly shared in Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn. You can even have an article about your local BNI group. What a great way to promote that in LinkedIn. Typing your twitter account into your LinkedIn profile allows you to connect your Twitter profile easily through LinkedIn.

One more. Building and enhancing credibility could very well be one of the first places that some people who don’t know you outside of BNI may look at to determine a little bit about you and get your business profile, which is again, one of the things that we talked about with BNI Connect recently. Finish your profile so people can find out about you. But LinkedIn allows a professional to showcase their works, their publications, recommendations from other LinkedIn users, company affiliations, work history, etc.

So the more you can have about yourself online, the more credibility that you have in business. There is really, I think, a lot of value in that. So the five things that I recommend for getting the most out of LinkedIn- and many of these overlap into other social media networking including BNI Connect are:
connecting with more people, participating in the groups, capitalizing on search engine optimization, tying in with a Twitter account and building and enhancing your credibility. I think these five things will help our members get more exposure though LinkedIn. Of course, when you are on there, you should put that you are a member of a local BNI chapter. That would be great to see more and more people do that.

So that is what I’ve got for today, Priscilla. Are you on LinkedIn?

Priscilla:
Yes, yes. I’m on LinkedIn. I read your article on this topic and I did something that I hadn’t done before., which is there is press release that came out today in SF Gate, which is a San Francisco Chronicle website, about the studio and one of my clients. I posted it on LinkedIn, which I had never done before. That was kind of cool. Anyway, I see the value of it and I appreciate that you pointed out all of these different tips for us.

Ivan:
I talk about in networking that it’s really a multitouch program. You have to connect with people in many different ways and this is one of the ways that I think is valuable. I have a LinkedIn profile. I recommend it to others. It, along with other forms of connecting and building credibility, can help other people build a powerful personal network. Thanks Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Thank you, Ivan. I think that is it for this week. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

There’s an official BNI group there, where members can share ideas with each other. Here are some ways you can get more out of LinkedIn. Connect with more people through your second and third level connections. Participate in […]

title.rendered: Episode 355: Promote a Fellow Member (Get Connected–Stay Connected, Part 8)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

John Meyer returns to the podcast to continue the “Get Connected–Stay Connected” series. Participation in BNI Connect has been increasing, so the campaign is having an effect.

Today’s topic is giving testimonials on BNI Connect. John would like to start by clearing up the difference between a thank you and a testimonial. Everyone understands the importance of saying “thank you” for referrals, but not everyone understands the definition of a testimonial. A testimonial is an explanation of why somebody should do business with a person. The stories that you see on SuccessNet under “I’m Connected” include many examples of testimonials for BNI.

Testimonials that you write for fellow BNI members appear on their BNI Connect profiles and are visible to the public.

Struggling to find the time to get your testimonials posted? Have your chapter schedule a BNI Connect testimonial week.

Every time a member gives a testimonial during a chapter meeting, have the leadership team prompt the person who gives the testimonial to add that testimonial to BNI Connect.

Brought to you by The Global Networking Show.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 355 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by the Global Networking Show, which is hosted by Andy Lopata and Dr. Ivan Misner. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi Priscilla. I am in Germany this week doing events for BNI Germany. What a great group! They all like my wife better than me, which I guess isn’t too uncommon, but she speaks German fluently so she gets along real well when an American can speak the language. We are doing great.

By the way, the Global Networking Show is a new sponsor for the BNI Podcast. I urge listeners to go check out the Global Networking Show. Andy Lopata and I do a show once a month on Google Hangout and we bring in experts from all over the world. We will invite Andy to a podcast, Priscilla and do a podcast with him.

This week, I have gotten back an- I was going to say an old friend but John is not that old. A long term friend of BNI Podcast, John Meyer. John probably has the record for being my guest more than anyone else on BNIpodcast.com. He is the US National Director for BNI. He has over 6200 chapters under his management. I’m sorry, how many members do you have now under your management?

John:
About 3800.

Ivan:
3800 members under your management. 6700 chapters now worldwide and John is in the Toledo, Ohio area. He is also a contributing author to Masters of Networking and loves golf. I could do a whole podcast on you talking about golf.

John:
I’m in!

Ivan:
Alright. But today, we are here to continue the series, Get Connected, Stay Connected, which was John and his team’s idea.

John, it seems to be working pretty well. Participation is going in the right direction. It is going up, which is good in this organization.

John:
It really has. We did a study to see the impact of the Get Connected, Stay Connected campaign earlier this year. It is truly amazing the impact. At least, we are thinking that is the impact of the usage of BNI Connect and how many members are utilizing the program. The word is truly spreading how powerful this program is.

Ivan:
It really is and for the first time in the history of this company, it is a global network. You can connect with people all over the world via BNI Connect. This month’s topic for Get Connected, Stay Connected is “Promote a Fellow Member.” Do you want to talk about that a little bit so that people can get an idea of what we would like you to focus on in BNI Connect this month?

John:
I would. Before I get into the actual topic, I want to talk real briefly on the definition of a thank you versus a testimonial because I think there may still be some confusion and I think you see that or hear about that in your travels as well, where people understand the importance of a thank you but they don’t understand the definition of a testimonial.

A testimonial truly is an explanation or a promotion of why somebody should do business with somebody else versus a thank you of thank you for giving me a referral or whatever and a nice bottle of burgundy or some kind of bottle of wine or a gift when somebody gives you a referral.

The first thing is we don’t want to confuse a thank you for a testimonial. However, in this topic, we want to focus on the testimonial aspect and how you can incorporate that in BNI Connect. We have the testimonials in the chapters. Now we want to take it a step further and make it visible to all BNI members around the world.

Ivan:
You know, we have asked forever since BNI started, practically, for people to share their testimonials at a BNI meeting. Now we are suggesting an additional thing. In addition to doing at the meeting, put it in writing and put it on BNI Connect because it will show up on a member’s profile. Isn’t that right?

John:
It will. It is interesting that you mentioned earlier that we truly are now a global network thanks to BNI Connect. The aspect of SuccessNet, where we have the “I’m Connected” stories, we start to see members connecting with other members around the world on referrals that are being passed.

So this aspect of Get Connected, Stay Connected-: Promote a Fellow Member- ties directly into the success stories that we are starting to see on SuccessNet. Now they can take those stories, put them onto that member’s actual profile in BNI Connect that all members get to see.

So when you talk about a global network, this truly falls into place with that philosophy.

Ivan:
It does and the profiles are visible to the public. So having testimonials on your profile certainly can help prospective buyers of products or services see the kinds of things that you do in relation to how other BNI members feel about your services, which I think is pretty important for most people.

John:
It really is. And it is fun to watch the testimonial build when people start to get involved in writing up or publishing the testimonials on BNI Connect. Pull up a couple people’s profiles and you will see four, five, six, ten, twelve different testimonials from different people who have used this member’s services.

Ivan:
How easy is it to post a testimonial? I know you are not in the role that Jeremy Walsh is in, and he can just tell you where to point, but I have done it. It’s pretty easy. Do you want to tell people about where they would go and what they would do?

Jeremy:
Actually, part of the BNI Connect program is connecting with people, so if the connection that you have made- you are automatically connected with all the members in your chapter. If you just click on their profile, there is a way that you can click on “Give a Testimonial”.

You can write up your description. I believe it is limited in the number of characters. You can’t write a War and Peace novel about the experience, but you can definitely provide a very nice Reader’s Digest version of how people or why people should do business with the person whom you are giving a testimonial for, which is really easy.

Finding the person’s information. Clicking on the “Give a Testimonial” button. Filling in the blanks.

Ivan:
I have given several testimonials. It is pretty easy to do. You actually do have plenty of space. You are actually right. It can’t be War and Peace, but you have plenty of space to deep and thorough of why they did such a good job.

I think the truth is that all of our members know that testimonials are really important. It is just a matter of taking the time to do it, so one of the things that I recommend if you are listening to this podcast, go back to your chapter and as a chapter, schedule a week that is BNI Connect testimonial week. This week, every member is going to post at least one testimonial for one other member. This week, every member. What do you think of that idea?

John:
I love that idea. That starts to drive the chapter to get involved, and that is ultimately what we are looking for. I think what you can do on top of what we just said is during the chapter meeting, have the president, vice president, secretary, member- ever time a member stands up to give a testimonial in the chapter, write down who gave the testimonial and who the testimonial is about and target them to put that on BNI Connect.

Ivan:
Great suggestion.

John:
It happens in the meeting. So let’s just move that to the BNI Connect site as well.

Ivan:
Yeah. In addition to as opposed to in place of.

John:
Exactly.

Ivan:
We are out of time today, John. For the listeners, John is joining me most month, not ever month, but many months the first week of every month for the Get Connected, Stay Connected podcasts that we are doing for 12 months in a row. This is, I think, number seven or eight or something like that.

It is great to have you on again, John, and we will be talking to you again next month.

Priscilla:
Okay great. I love that idea. I am going to take it back to my chapter this week. Thank you so much for listening. I would like to just remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Global Networking Show, which is hosted by Andy Lopata and Dr. Ivan Misner. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

John Meyer explains how to promote a fellow member with a BNI Connect testimonial.

title.rendered: Episode 354: Four Things to Know about Your Business

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Here are four things you should know about your business. If you don’t know the answers to these questions, it will be very difficult for people to refer you, because you won’t have communicated these key points to them.

  1. What is your mission? What are you striving for?
  2. Where is your organization going? How can you improve your chances of achieving your mission?
  3. What environment is your organization operating in? What are the trends affecting you?
  4. What are your core competencies? What do you do better than your competitors?

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 354 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing great, Priscilla. Thank you. Today we are going to talk about four things that I believe that everyone needs to know about their business. So what I am going to do is pull out my old professor’s hat and put it on. Okay, I didn’t really have a professor’s hat, but metaphorically, I am going to pull it out, dust it off and share some things that I think every business that wants to be successful really needs to think about in order to remain successful or become successful.

There are four questions, and if you are listening to this podcast, just listen. You don’t have to take notes. Just look at the transcript because the transcript will have all of this information. So for now, just process it. Think about it as I speak.

The first question that I think you need to ask- and oh, by the way, this applies in a networking context because if you don’t know the answers to these points, it is very difficult for other people to refer you because you are not communicating some of these key points to them. The first one is, what is my business?

What is your business? “Yeah, I am simply making a living.” What are your long range professional goals? Do you wish to become the standard that your competitors are judged by? Is it your dream to improve the workplace or improve people’s lives? What is it? What is your mission? What are you all about as a company and as an organization.

Remember, a mission is something that you’re striving for. It is something that you may not ever achieve, but is it a journey, not a destination. BNI’s mission is “changing the way the world does business.” Will we ever change the way the entire world does business? I would like to think it’s possible, but it’s one of the more lofty goals that may not ever be fully achievable, but the more you achieve it, the more successful you are. What is your mission?

The second is where is my organization going? Am I achieving my mission? Am I making plans to accomplish my mission? How can I change my policies, my procedures, personnel that improve my chances of achieving my mission?

There are a lot of ways to do this. You can do a SWOT analysis of your business. A SWOT analysis is where you sit down and take a look at your company’s strengths, weaknesses, opportunities and threats. That’s SWOT. Strengths, weaknesses, opportunities and threats.

One of the things that I recommend is that if you have people who work for you, have them write those down as well. Come up with a simple list of short phrases. Not a master’s thesis but just short phrases. One word or sentence on the strengths, weaknesses, opportunities and threats.

Come up with a pretty exhaustive list. One of the things that I recommend is that you create a SurveyMonkey survey. You don’t have to rank order but just give us a list of all of our strengths, all of our weakness, opportunities and threats. And then go to SurveyMonkey and put them all up.

Tell people to mark the ones that apply most. Tell people to pick the top three strengths of the company or the top five strengths of the company, but limit the number. Then you put it all together and you get a really good picture from the employees, or if you are a really big company maybe just the management, of what are the top five or ten strengths of the company? What are the top five or ten weaknesses of the company? It is a great way to help figure out where you are going as an organization.

The third thing to know about your organization is what environment is my organization operating in? What are the social, economic and technological trends that affect the way that you are doing business and the progress toward your goals?

It might be regulation that is impacting you. It might be the economy or competition. What is the environment that you are in? And understand it.

The fourth is a really important one and that is what are my core competencies? What do I like to do? What is it that I do better than my competitors? Is my business mission compatible with my values and my skill set?

You want to think about it in two ways. One, in core competencies as a company, a business. And what are your own personal core competencies?

Ideally, as your business grows, you want to be focusing on your core competencies as the organization gets bigger and bigger and have other people focus on those aspects of your business that aren’t really your strength.

So I would love to chat a little bit about this, Priscilla, if you have any questions. Before we do, I have another thought and that is I have seen too many business professionals in other companies make the mistake of trying to be all things to all people.

Starting out with a fundamentally sound goal of finding a niche that will make them successful- instead of finding that niche, they kind of go astray by changing direction every time a customer or associate suggests a new product or service. The mission gets lost in a frantic scramble for business before the original idea really gets a chance to pay off.

So even if you think you know your mission, I believe it will serve you well to pause periodically and analyze your business and sort of re-focus back on your mission and your philosophy.

Priscilla:
I think that’s great. The one thing that I would like to ask you about is adaptability because sometimes when technology changes or trends change in your business, then you do need to incorporate aspects into your business that you haven’t before and kind of broaden out in order not to be eliminated or go out of business. What do you think about that?

Ivan:
That is absolutely correct. It’s not mutually exclusive from what I am talking about. For example, BNI’s mission is to help people in a weekly structured word of mouth referral marketing program. So that has been our mission- that has been part of our process for many, many years. Our mission being changing the way the world does business and then doing it through the structured word of mouth marketing program.

So as long as we are on mission with that strategy, how we get there may vary. 30 years ago, technology was not a factor. Today, technology is. So does technology help us change the way the world does business, and does it help us increase members in a structured referral marketing program? Yes.

We are still on mission and on focus as an organization, but it is something new and something different. Does that make sense?

Priscilla:
Yeah, yeah. It does. I was thinking more, for example, in the field of photography, people aren’t spending as much money taking fine photographs as they did in the past because everybody has a cell phone or some kind of digital technology. So a photographer has to sort of take that into consideration and broaden what they can offer and try to maintain their professional desirability, so to speak.

Ivan:
Yes, but I look at photographers that are successful today and they are doing it with online technology. They are taking photographs and making those photographs available online in any number of ways. You can either download a digital version of the photograph that they took or you can order prints online. So that is a much different way of doing business.

You know, you can’t put your head in the sand and say, “I don’t like the fact the people can get photos digitally.” You may not like it but it’s just the world today. So embrace it and find ways to make money from it.

You know, iTunes certainly did that with the technology of music. There was a period when people were saying music and the recording industry was dying because of the technology. Well, in fact, technology can help create a boom for industries if you find ways to embrace it.

By the way, I should clarify something. It has been too long since I have been a professor. I mixed up mission and vision. Our vision as an organization is changing the way the world does business. Our mission in BNI is to help people in a structured word of mouth referral marketing program. That is our mission. There is a difference between mission and vision.

Priscilla:
Great. Okay.

Ivan:
Does that answer your comment? What we are talking about is what is known as adaptive capacity. Dr. Warren Bennis calls it adaptive capacity, your ability to adapt to a changing time and to apply your mission and to apply your vision within the context of that, adapting into that new technology or new situation.

Priscilla:
Right. Yeah, yeah. That does. Flexibility, I think, is what we are referring to.

Ivan:
Great. That is all I have for today. Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Thank you, Dr. Misner. Well, I would like to just remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard
